params.text:
Equality, diversity and inclusion in care

Every person you support is different, and those differences deserve respect. Standard 4 of the Care Certificate asks you to put equality, diversity and inclusion (often shortened to EDI) at the heart of your practice. These three ideas are closely linked, but they are not the same thing.

  • Equality means making sure people have fair access to the same rights, opportunities and quality of care. Importantly, equality is not about treating everyone identically – it is about giving each person what they need so they can achieve a fair outcome.
  • Diversity means recognising and valuing the many ways in which people differ – in culture, background, beliefs, abilities, identity and life experience. Diversity treats difference as a strength, not a problem.
  • Inclusion means making sure people feel welcomed, valued and able to take part fully, rather than being left out or treated as an afterthought.

When you get EDI right, you are not just following the law – you are delivering warmer, more personal, more dignified care.

params.question: Equality means treating every person you support in exactly the same way.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Not quite. Treating everyone identically can be unfair – true equality means giving each person what they need to reach a fair outcome.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. Equality is about fairness of outcome, so it often means adapting your support to each person's individual needs rather than treating everyone identically.
